modules.title: Module 1: TypeScript Fundamentals Refresher
modules.description: Review core TypeScript concepts and tooling setup.
objectives: Refresh knowledge of TypeScript basics (types, interfaces, generics)
Set up a professional development environment (TSConfig, ESLint, Prettier)
Understand the TypeScript compiler (tsc) and its configuration

modules.title: Module 2: Advanced Type System Deep Dive
modules.description: Explore TypeScript's advanced type system features.
objectives: Master union/intersection types and discriminated unions
Learn type guards, type assertions, and the `unknown` type
Understand mapped, conditional, and recursive types

modules.title: Module 3: Object-Oriented Programming in TypeScript
modules.description: Implement OOP patterns and principles with TypeScript.
objectives: Use classes, access modifiers, and abstract classes effectively
Implement interfaces for contracts and dependency injection
Explore SOLID principles in TypeScript

modules.title: Module 4: Functional Programming with TypeScript
modules.description: Adopt functional programming paradigms in TypeScript.
objectives: Work with immutability, pure functions, and higher-order functions
Use utility types (Pick, Omit, Partial, etc.)
Implement currying and function composition

modules.title: Module 5: Asynchronous Programming & Error Handling
modules.description: Handle async operations and errors robustly.
objectives: Master async/await, Promises, and TypeScript's `void` type
Implement proper error handling with custom error types
Use TypeScript with Node.js streams and event emitters

modules.title: Module 6: Advanced Patterns & Design Techniques
modules.description: Apply professional design patterns and techniques.
objectives: Implement factory, singleton, and decorator patterns
Use dependency injection and inversion of control
Leverage the visitor pattern for complex type transformations

modules.title: Module 7: TypeScript with Modern Frameworks
modules.description: Integrate TypeScript with popular frameworks and tools.
objectives: Use TypeScript with React, Angular, or Vue for frontend development
Integrate TypeScript with backend frameworks (Express, NestJS)
Explore TypeScript with testing frameworks (Jest, Cypress)

modules.title: Module 8: Performance Optimization & Best Practices
modules.description: Optimize TypeScript code for performance and maintainability.
objectives: Profile and optimize TypeScript applications
Implement type-safe APIs (REST, GraphQL, gRPC)
Adopt best practices for large-scale TypeScript projects

modules.title: Module 1: TypeScript Fundamentals Refresher
modules.content:
## Module 1: TypeScript Fundamentals Refresher

Welcome to the first module of *Learn TypeScript for Professional Development*. In this chapter, we’ll refresh your foundational knowledge of TypeScript and set up a professional development environment. Whether you’re transitioning from JavaScript or revisiting TypeScript after some time, this module will ensure you’re grounded in the essentials before diving into advanced topics.

---

## Why TypeScript?

TypeScript is a **typed superset of JavaScript** that compiles to plain JavaScript. It adds static types, classes, and modules to JavaScript, making your code more predictable, maintainable, and scalable—especially in large applications.

For professional development, TypeScript helps:
- Catch errors during development (not in production).
- Improve code readability with explicit types.
- Enable better tooling (autocompletion, refactoring).
- Support modern JavaScript features while maintaining broad compatibility.

---

## Core TypeScript Concepts

Let’s review the foundational building blocks of TypeScript.

### 1. Types and Interfaces

**Types** and **interfaces** are two ways to define the shape of your data.

#### Type Aliases
A `type` defines a new name for a type.

```typescript
type UserId = string | number;

function getUser(id: UserId): User {
  // Implementation...
}
```

#### Interfaces
An `interface` defines the structure of an object.

```typescript
interface User {
  id: UserId;
  name: string;
  email: string;
  isActive?: boolean; // Optional property
}

function printUser(user: User): void {
  console.log(`${user.name} (${user.email})`);
}
```

**Key differences:**
- Interfaces can be extended (`extends`) and implemented (`implements`).
- Types can define unions, tuples, and more complex structures.

```typescript
interface AdminUser extends User {
  role: "admin";
}

type Point = [number, number]; // Tuple
```

---

### 2. Generics

Generics allow you to write reusable, type-safe components.

```typescript
function identity<T>(arg: T): T {
  return arg;
}

const num = identity<number>(10); // T is number
const str = identity<string>("hello"); // T is string
```

**Real-world example: A reusable API response handler**

```typescript
interface ApiResponse<T> {
  data: T;
  status: number;
  message?: string;
}

function fetchData<T>(url: string): Promise<ApiResponse<T>> {
  return fetch(url).then(res => res.json());
}

// Usage
fetchData<User[]>("/api/users").then(response => {
  console.log(response.data[0].name); // Type-safe access
});
```

Generics are powerful for creating flexible yet type-safe abstractions.

---

### 3. Type Inference and Union Types

TypeScript can often infer types automatically.

```typescript
let message = "Hello"; // Inferred as `string`
message = 123; // Error: Type 'number' is not assignable to type 'string'
```

**Union types** allow a variable to be one of several types.

```typescript
type Status = "active" | "inactive" | "pending";

function updateStatus(status: Status): void {
  // ...
}

updateStatus("active"); // OK
updateStatus("deleted"); // Error: Type '"deleted"' is not assignable to type 'Status'
```

---

## Setting Up a Professional Development Environment

A professional TypeScript setup goes beyond writing `.ts` files. Let’s configure tooling for linting, formatting, and compilation.

### 1. Initialize a TypeScript Project

```bash
npm init -y
npm install typescript --save-dev
npx tsc --init
```

This generates a `tsconfig.json` file—the heart of your TypeScript configuration.

---

### 2. Understanding `tsconfig.json`

Edit the `tsconfig.json` to match your project needs:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2020",
    "module": "commonjs",
    "outDir": "./dist",
    "rootDir": "./src",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules"]
}
```

**Key options:**
- `target`: Specifies ECMAScript version.
- `module`: Module system (e.g., `commonjs`, `esnext`).
- `outDir`: Where compiled JS goes.
- `rootDir`: Source directory.
- `strict`: Enables all strict type-checking options.
- `esModuleInterop`: Improves compatibility with CommonJS modules.

Run the compiler:
```bash
npx tsc
```

This compiles `src/*.ts` into `dist/*.js`.

---

### 3. Adding ESLint and Prettier

Linting and formatting ensure code quality and consistency.

#### Install dependencies:
```bash
npm install eslint @typescript-eslint/parser @typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin prettier eslint-config-prettier eslint-plugin-prettier --save-dev
```

#### Create `.eslintrc.json`:
```json
{
  "root": true,
  "parser": "@typescript-eslint/parser",
  "plugins": ["@typescript-eslint"],
  "extends": [
    "eslint:recommended",
    "plugin:@typescript-eslint/recommended",
    "plugin:@typescript-eslint/stylistic",
    "prettier"
  ],
  "rules": {
    "@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any": "warn",
    "@typescript-eslint/explicit-module-boundary-types": "off"
  }
}
```

#### Create `.prettierrc`:
```json
{
  "semi": true,
  "singleQuote": true,
  "tabWidth": 2,
  "trailingComma": "es5"
}
```

#### Add scripts to `package.json`:
```json
"scripts": {
  "lint": "eslint 'src/**/*.ts'",
  "format": "prettier --write 'src/**/*.ts'"
}
```

Run linting:
```bash
npm run lint
```

Format code:
```bash
npm run format
```

---

### 4. TypeScript Compiler (tsc) Workflow

The TypeScript compiler (`tsc`) does more than just transpile:
- **Type checking**: Catches errors before runtime.
- **Declaration generation**: Creates `.d.ts` files for type definitions.
- **Source map generation**: Helps debug TypeScript in browser/dev tools.

Use `tsc --watch` in development for live compilation.

---

## Common Pitfalls and Pro Tips

- **Don’t over-annotate**: Let TypeScript infer types when possible.
- **Use `unknown` instead of `any`**: It forces type checking.
- **Enable `noImplicitAny`**: Catches implicit `any` types.
- **Avoid `as` assertions**: Use type guards instead for runtime safety.

---

## Example: Full Module Integration

Let’s tie it all together in a small module.

**src/user.service.ts**

```typescript
interface User {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  age?: number;
}

class UserService {
  private users: User[] = [];

  addUser(user: User): void {
    this.users.push(user);
  }

  findUser(id: string): User | undefined {
    return this.users.find(u => u.id === id);
  }
}

export default UserService;
```

**tsconfig.json** ensures this compiles correctly.

**ESLint** flags unused variables or missing types.

**Prettier** formats all files consistently.

---

## Summary

In this module, we:
- Reviewed TypeScript types, interfaces, and generics.
- Set up a professional dev environment with `tsconfig.json`, ESLint, and Prettier.
- Learned how to use the TypeScript compiler effectively.

You now have a solid foundation to build upon.

---

## Key Takeaways

- TypeScript adds static types to JavaScript, improving reliability and developer experience.
- Use `interfaces` for object shapes and `types` for unions, tuples, or complex types.
- Generics enable reusable, type-safe functions and classes.
- A professional setup includes `tsconfig.json`, ESLint, and Prettier for consistency and correctness.
